topic: Someone finds out their best friend is moving across the country
raw_output:
[story 1]

The map spread across the coffee table seemed to mock me. Every city line, every state boundary, was a jagged tear in the familiar landscape of our shared history. I traced the route from here to Denver with a fingertip, the paper crinkling under the pressure. "Seriously?" I finally managed, the word coming out thin and brittle. Liam just shrugged, fiddling with the edge of his worn backpack. His eyes, usually so bright and full of easy laughter, looked distant, as if already scanning a horizon I wasn't privy to. A knot tightened low in my stomach, a cold, hard pressure that made my jaw ache. I stood up abruptly, pacing the small apartment floor, my feet tapping an uneven rhythm against the wood. I needed movement, needed something to break the static hum in my chest, anything to displace this suffocating stillness.

[story 2]

I kept catching myself staring out the window, watching the familiar streetlights blur into streaks of amber. Every time I turned, I saw him—Liam, leaning against the doorframe, talking animatedly about packing lists and new apartment layouts. The details were too sharp, too concrete, solidifying the enormity of the change. It wasn't just a change of address; it felt like a tectonic shift. I picked up my phone, intending to text him, but my thumb hovered over the screen, paralyzed. My hands started to tremble slightly, a faint vibration that seemed to originate deep in my core. I walked into the kitchen, running water until the spray hit my face, trying to shock the sluggish feeling out of my limbs. I needed to *do* something, to burn off this internal energy that felt too big to contain.

[story 3]

The silence in the room amplified the sound of my own breathing. I kept rearranging the books on the shelf, pushing them back into alignment, over and over. It was a pointless task, a frantic attempt to impose order on a situation that was inherently chaotic. When Liam finally confirmed the move, his voice was surprisingly even, which only made my internal agitation spike higher. I felt a sudden, sharp urge to grab my coat and just *leave*, to run until the pavement blurred beneath my shoes. Instead, I just sat on the edge of the sofa, swinging my legs, watching the dust motes dance in the afternoon sunbeam slicing across the floor. My skin felt too tight, buzzing with an unspent kinetic energy, demanding an outlet that the quiet afternoon refused to provide.
